chunk_summary: This section outlines methods for documenting breastfeeding effectiveness and follow-up metrics, emphasizing structured assessments, standardized tools, and actionable documentation to prevent complications like dehydration and hyperbilirubinemia.
learning_objectives: Understand the components of a structured breastfeeding assessment, including maternal and infant factors.
Learn to use standardized tools like the LATCH score without substituting them for clinical judgment.
Identify key follow-up metrics for high-risk dyads, including weight change, hydration, and jaundice progression.
Develop actionable documentation plans to support exclusive breastfeeding and reduce preventable readmissions.
key_facts: Structured feed observation includes maternal comfort, latch quality, audible swallowing, and infant alertness.
Follow-up schedules should be earlier for high-risk dyads, such as late preterm or post-cesarean infants.
Key metrics include infant weight change, hydration status, jaundice progression, and maternal breast/nipple condition.
Supplementation plans must document indication, volume, method, and tapering strategy.
Actionable documentation reduces preventable readmissions for dehydration and hyperbilirubinemia.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Linking documentation to action reduces preventable readmissions for dehydration and hyperbilirubinemia and supports exclusive breastfeeding at home.
